vacuum_prediction.reasoning: The tied-balls thought experiment reveals an internal contradiction in Aristotle's thesis: if heavier falls faster, then binding a heavy and light ball together must simultaneously make the composite faster (more total weight) and slower (the lighter ball retards the heavier).

This contradiction implies that weight alone cannot be the determinant of fall speed. The observed speed differences in everyday experience must therefore stem from an external factor — the resistance of the surrounding medium.
Extrapolating to the limit of zero medium resistance (vacuum), the confounding factor vanishes entirely, so objects of different weights must fall at the same rate.
